title: Manga And what of Sadamotos Manga
display_title: Manga And what of Sadamotos Manga
wikitext:
[]: [Manga] And what of Sadamoto's Manga?             Quote Post by pwhodges » Thu Jul 11, 2013 4:55 pm As the thread in which announcements of the progress of Sadamoto's Evangelion manga were posted and discussed has been closed, clearly a new thread is required. After all, the original anime and the film series each have a whole forum in which they are discussed; and here is a third continuity, sitting in some respects (not just publication dates) between those two, written by an important member of the team for both of them - at the very least it should have a thread in which the way it tells the story can be considered, discussed and argued about. First, I imagine there is little disagreement that the art itself is superb from one end to the other; nothing much to discuss there - and being drawn by the designer of the TV and film shows, it's only to be expected that the designs are pretty much the same throughout. But what of the characterisation and story? Are they in some respects improved by following the experience of producing the show? Or are they more weakened by being ultimately the product of a single mind which is not Anno's? And as a result, where does it sit in respect of canonicity? Is the ending weak or strong - and what underlies people seeing it one way or the other? Did the changes in the manga influence the movies, or is that a case of parallel but independent development?

[]: Re: And what of Sadamoto's Manga?             Quote Post by SimplyMason0 » Thu Jul 11, 2013 8:14 pm The art is well liked from what I heard. The plot and characters don't have much of the flesh and deepness as the anime. Shinji is totally different as he is a smart alec than the iconic shy kid. Kaworu is in it more after Shinji gets absorb Rei gets more focus Asuka doesn't have her drama with Shinji and they are much more friendly with each other Misato also loses a lot of her stuff too Its inferior to the anime but its a decent read. Its different sure but its better than the same thing. Its not ment to compete with the anime. The ending is crap. I don't think there is stuff regarding rebuild but it has the same type of quality 2.22 had I guess

[]: Re: And what of Sadamoto's Manga?             Quote Post by TruthfulLie » Fri Jul 12, 2013 1:06 am SimplyMason0 wrote: The plot and characters don't have much of the flesh and deepness as the anime.

[]: Re: And what of Sadamoto's Manga?             Quote Post by SimplyMason0 » Fri Jul 12, 2013 2:41 am TruthfulLie wrote: I don't know if I agree to that. They are simply different, not necessarily shallower than anime counterpart.

[]:             Quote Post by Bhorium » Fri Jul 12, 2013 6:42 pm So I guess this is the tropic in which to do a post-mortem on the manga ending? I have already mentioned that I didn't like it, so I felt like giving some more in-depth reasons: SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Jul 12, 2013 9:26 pm ^ Agreed 100% That said, there's no reason people have to restrict themselves to just talking about the ending. Surely the official manga is at least half as worthy as the series of some discussion. TBH, it would be nice if people started new threads on such topics rather than feeling like everything manga-related has to go all into one.

[]:             Quote Post by TruthfulLie » Fri Jul 12, 2013 11:18 pm Regardless of the ending itself, I quite like the comic adaptation for few reasons. I'll be honest and I initially did think that it was a bit of dumbed down version of the series and was quick to judge that it was far less engaging and inferior to the original, but his interpretations on some of the minor characters gave more life to them which is something I enjoyed a lot. Gendo's last moment, Yui's last promise to Shinji, Fuyutsuki's final confession to Yui and last moment between Shinji and Rei were the some of the parts I think was very well rendered into the event of Third Impact. So after all is said and done, I came to like it a lot more than I have before and decide to accept it as it is. I don't necessarily think that Sadamoto's conclusion completely goes against that Anno had shown us with EoE. That's not to say that I don't have any criticism either. I do think that Sadamoto's ending is bit naïve and obtuse approach that is in disarray by offering some kind of restart of the world. The problem is that by doing so, he has made the message rather watered down. Instead of showing the harsh truth of the reality, he has offered a beautiful illusion where things are back to normal and that Shinji has been given a second chance. He may not be aware of the events that took place on conscious level, but I do believe that he does on his subconscious level based on change of heart and that he seems to respond to the ruins with particular manner. But finally the reason I don't despise its ending despite the fact it's not as keen and cunning as Anno's take, is because I believe that it's simply another way of telling the message. To be quite frank I did think and still do think that conclusion of EoE is so shockingly brutal that it may not be the best effective way of conveying the idea to a wide audience. There is certain a feeling of despair when knowing that there is such tiny hope in a grim reality that it may actually cause repulsive reaction which I actually have witnessed. Which is ironic because that's the very reason that EoE's ending was so brilliant in the first place. It shows that it't not going to be all sunshine and rainbow in such a brutal manner that gave you chill. But again, that's also the very reason someone might take it the wrong way. As much as most of us (including myself) here may find Anno's approach more appealing, there a lot of people who do not. You can tell this by observing the reaction from someone who is outside of Evangelion fandom community. It's not much of stretch to say that many people miss the hopeful side of things in conclusion of EoE. I think what Sadamoto has done, offers another approach. It's bit dull and sugar coated, but perhaps it works for some who didn't find Anno's conclusion appealing.

[]:             Quote Post by Bhorium » Sat Jul 13, 2013 8:59 am I guess my main beef with the manga over all is that it introduces all these changes to the storyline and the characters, yet seems afraid to commit to and build upon them. The ending could really just be seen as the ultimate expression of this. To elaborate: It is not only the ultimately inconsequential death of Toji that gives off that vibe. Another example is the twist on Gendo's character. The glimpses behind his mask are reduced and downplayed, and his villainous traits is played up to the point where he is an outright clacking madman, and his true motivation isn't "Get Yui back, no matter the cost!", but, to paraphrase what he says to Shinji near the end; "I'm trying to destroy the world because I'm a dick!" So again, it rings a little hollow when the manga tries to pull the same kind of redemption as EoE for him in the 11th hour, after going to such lengths to remove his humanity.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Jul 13, 2013 4:56 pm I thought Gendo's plan was "I'll become god so I never have to lose anything I care about again."

[]:             Quote Post by Bhorium » Sat Jul 13, 2013 6:30 pm I was mostly thinking of the line he punctuates his Al-Pacino-in-The-Devil's-Avocate tirade with: If you hate this world like me, join me in seeking revenge.

[]:             Quote Post by SimplyMason0 » Sat Jul 13, 2013 9:31 pm Bhorium wrote: Gendo Goal
